urlState.title: URL State with nuqs
urlState.description: nuqs provides type-safe URL search params state management for React. State lives in the URL, making it shareable, bookmarkable, and SSR-friendly.
urlState.sectionLiveDemoDesc: Try the controls below and watch the URL update in real-time. Every state change is reflected in search params.
urlState.sectionSetupDesc: nuqs requires an adapter in your providers for Next.js App Router.
urlState.sectionParsersDesc: nuqs includes built-in parsers for common types. They validate and transform URL strings automatically.
urlState.sectionUsageDesc: useQueryState works like useState but syncs with URL search params.
urlState.keyTypeSafeDesc: Parsers validate and transform URL params. parseAsInteger ensures you always get a number, not a string.
urlState.keyShareableDesc: State lives in the URL. Copy the URL and paste it elsewhere — the exact same state is restored.
urlState.keyDefaultsDesc: withDefault() provides fallback values. When a param is missing from the URL, the default is used instead of null.
urlState.keySSRFriendlyDesc: Server Components can read searchParams directly. nuqs handles client-side hydration seamlessly with no mismatch.

lazyLoading.title: Lazy Loading (next/dynamic)
lazyLoading.description: next/dynamic lets you defer loading components until they are needed, reducing the initial JavaScript bundle size.
lazyLoading.sectionLiveDemoDesc: The heavy component below is loaded dynamically. It was not included in the initial page bundle.
lazyLoading.sectionBasicUsageDesc: Import components dynamically with next/dynamic. They are code-split automatically.
lazyLoading.sectionNoSSRDesc: Some components (e.g., those using browser APIs) must be rendered client-side only.
lazyLoading.sectionCustomLoadingDesc: Pass a loading component to show while the dynamic component is being fetched.
lazyLoading.sectionImportPatternDesc: Under the hood, next/dynamic uses React.lazy + Suspense with dynamic import().
lazyLoading.keyCodeSplittingDesc: Each dynamic() call creates a separate chunk. The component is only downloaded when it is first rendered.
lazyLoading.keySSRControlDesc: Set ssr: false for components that use window, document, or other browser-only APIs. They will render only on the client.
lazyLoading.keyLoadingStateDesc: The loading option lets you show a placeholder while the component loads. Great for skeleton UIs.
lazyLoading.keyNamedExportsDesc: Use the arrow function form to import named exports: dynamic(() => import('./mod').then(m => m.MyComponent)).

searchParams.title: searchParams (Promise-based)
searchParams.description: In Next.js 16, searchParams is a Promise that must be awaited. This is a breaking change from earlier versions where it was a plain object.
searchParams.sectionLiveDemoDesc: Use the form below to add search params. The server component awaits and displays them.
searchParams.noParams: No search params. Use the form to add some!
searchParams.sectionV14VsV16Desc: The searchParams prop changed from a synchronous object to an async Promise.
searchParams.sectionServerUsageDesc: In Server Components, await the searchParams Promise directly.
searchParams.sectionClientUsageDesc: In Client Components, use the useSearchParams hook from next/navigation.
searchParams.keyPromiseDesc: searchParams is now a Promise in page/layout props. You must await it in Server Components or use React.use() in Client Components.
searchParams.keyBreakingDesc: In v14, searchParams was a plain object. In v16, it is a Promise. Forgetting to await will give you a Promise object, not params.
searchParams.keyMultipleValuesDesc: A key can have multiple values (e.g., ?color=red&color=blue). The value becomes a string array instead of a string.
searchParams.keyTypeSignatureDesc: The type is Promise<Record<string, string | string[] | undefined>>. Always handle the undefined case for optional params.
